topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice, stark white on a beige envelope, lay on my desk like a dead weight. I kept tracing the edges with my thumb, the paper feeling brittle beneath my touch. Every time I looked up, my eyes snagged on the faded university crest, suddenly feeling like a mocking symbol of a future that had just dissolved. I stood up abruptly, knocking my chair back with a loud scrape that echoed in the suddenly cavernous office. My hands were slick with a sudden clamminess, and I needed air, desperately. The silence of the administrative wing pressed in on me, thick and suffocating. "No," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy. "No, this isn't happening."

[story 2]

I stared at the campus map tacked above my cramped dorm room desk. The vibrant green of the quad seemed suddenly sickly under the fluorescent lights. I picked up my phone, intending to call a friend, but my fingers hovered over the screen, trembling slightly. The information had settled in my stomach like a cold stone, heavy and immovable. I paced the small space back and forth, my breath coming in shallow, quick gasps. Each step felt clumsy, as if my coordination had deserted me. I stopped by the window, pressing my forehead against the cool glass, trying to steady the frantic rhythm hammering against my ribs. The world outside seemed too bright, too solid, a cruel contrast to the internal wobble I couldn't control.

[story 3]

When the Dean finally called me into his office, the air felt thin, insufficient for the gravity of the news. I sat rigidly in the leather chair, my posture unnaturally stiff. When he spoke about the closure, his voice was measured, professional, but the words washed over me, creating a strange, hollow ringing in my ears. I kept fiddling with the hem of my sweater, a nervous habit I couldn't seem to break. I looked down at my hands, noticing the slight tremor that made holding a pen difficult. I tried to formulate a coherent response, but the words caught in my throat, turning to dust. I just stared at the mahogany desk, wishing the floor would simply open up and swallow me whole.
